title_guess: East Anglia: Peckover House, Fotheringay, Foulsham, Norfolk Broads, Halesworth

pages.text:
R. Severn. We could see the steam train across the water. Margaret was strong enough to walk quite a long way across the field & back.

__Saturday April 18th.__
Simon, Eve & Tonic set off eastwards. A[?t] was chilly, but gradually the sun broke through. We had lunch in the carpark near Peckover House, a very nice Georgian NT house. Built 1722 & lived in from 1796 by the Peckham family, Quaker bankers. We joined a tour of the garden. The Tulip tree has flat, -[?gottahed] leaves. There's also a quince tree & a ginks tree. Originated 65 mill. years ago (fossils found in coal seams). Really a [strikethrough: ten] fern. Within 4 months ginkos were found sprouting in the ruins of Hiroshima in 1944.

captions: The church of St. Mary + All Saints, Fotheringay!
pages.text:
Earlier in the day we had passed through Fotheringay, the village where Mary Queen of Scots was held captive. The Castle is no longer there, but the church is very impressive. There

[photo: illuminated church at night]

The church of St. Mary + All Saints, Fotheringay!

was [struck: at] a very chatty woman with a black dog.

****

__The Queen's Head__, __Foulsham__, where we are staying, has 5 pigs in a paddock at the end of the pub garden/ campsite. Tonic is bemused. [marginal annotation: lamb+mint / [?sct] pud.]

Horror of horrors! I had forgotten to bring extra loo paper. The cornershop where we stopped in the depths of Norfolk was manned by an Indian (via Kenya)

__Sunday__

Why is it that country people feel that they can be as rude as they like at the mention of "Birmingham?" Apart from that, the church, Holy Innocents, was very friendly. Children were at the Baptist church.

On to lunch in the carpark at Blakeney, which was by the sea according to the map, but infact we could [struck] scarcely see it because of mud flats. We walked but it was windy. Lots of birdspotters. In the end we drove inland – less traffic &

more sun. Small zigzag roads to Seapalling.

	Lots of ex-windmills.

We found a pleasant small campsite on The old bridge road in Potter Heigham. After opening the campingvan curtains in the afternoon we saw a group of Dutch campers who had come on a course about making rammed earth houses.

	In the evening we visited a boat yard on the river Yare. A company Sheerline who make river boats took a chad catalogue. We also went to a rural business park that was well designed and dropped catalogues at two interior design firms. It was then an hour to Halesworth, but we stopped just north at Spexhall and walked through the churchyard, across a field and stream up to a farm and back. Tonic showed a return of youthful energy. After lunch we picked up lampshades from WK Howarth and set off for Annette, briefly stopping to drop another catalogue on the way.

	Annette gave us tea in the garden in pleasant sunshine. We returned to 86 at about 7.45. Charles had already eate.
